speaker: interviewer
text: This is November 24, 1996. The survivor is Hyme Hipsman. Amy Tarr is the interviewer. We're in West Roxbury, Massachusetts, USA. And we are, of course, in English. Amy, go for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Amy Tarr, A-M-Y T-A-R-R, and I'm conducting an interview with Hyme Hipsman. It's November 24, 1996, and we are in West Roxbury, Massachusetts, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hipsman, can you tell us your name and spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want-- you want my name from now, or from before?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both. What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim, C-H-- C-H-A-I-M, Chaim Hipszman, H-I-P-S-Z-M-A-N. That was in Poland. In here, in the States, is my name is Hyme-- H-Y-M-E. And my name is H-I-P-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Wlodawa. 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Date-- date-- January 25, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Wlodawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wlodawa is a powiat-- [POLISH]-- this-- it's like Massachusetts state-- Lublin state.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean Lublin?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Wlodawa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wlodawa. Yes. W-L-O-D-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am-- I think I'll be 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Hipsman, can you tell us about your family before the war, in Wlodawa-- the other members of your family. Who were they? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two-- a sister after me. The name was Basha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basha, B-A-S-Z-A, Basha. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I had next, two brothers. One name was Yossel. And the other one was Zelig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yossel--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think Yossel-- I cannot-- in Poland, it was no spelling there, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- mother's name was Faige, F-A-I-G-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lew. L-E-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Mendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hipszman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mendel Hipszman. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me something about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- I don't know too much, but I know one thing. My father was a baker, and he worked for somebody. We didn't have our own bakery. He worked for a baker. And my mother was a housewife, took care in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they like as people, that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like people-- they were good Polish people. I mean, the-- the Jewish people, they-- not the rich one, were pretty good people, you know? They weren't too religious, but they were religious enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. We went every Saturday to shul, yes. Every Saturday, my father went to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Wlodawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wlodawa, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was called? The shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul? No, they were-- yeah. They called the old shul. Yeah. That's true. They had the old shul, yes. Yeah, we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like, the shul? Do you remember what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember the shul. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's really not a shul. It was a besmedresh. Cause we had a shul too there. And it was very big, you know? It took in a lot of people. And I remember doing [NON-ENGLISH] used to be in the mizrach [NON-ENGLISH] And they had a bema where they did-- they laid in the Torah. It was a very nice place to come in. And I remember, as a boy, every Saturday afternoon, I went in in the besmedresh, and I saw a lot of people was learning, learning Torah there. And I stayed, and I listened. So that was only Saturday afternoon. The maftir, or whatever they are, yes. It was very, very comfortable to come in there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the High Holidays at home at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure, I remember. The High Holidays is-- it was something special, that everybody-- for the High Holidays, everybody had to get a new suit, new shoes. And we all looked nice, and the house was-- it wasn't that-- it was only a two, two-room apartment, you know. But it cleaned up. It looks nice, you know? And that's what-- we didn't know no other way of life, except that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Wlodawa like when you were-- before the war when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, it was half Jewish and half Chri-- half gentile. And the Jews lived mostly close together. And the gentiles, they lived around the city because they had the land there, this and that, so that's what the-- that's, that's what I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Wlodawa in Poland? What other cities is it near?

speaker: interviewee
text: It near Chelm. It goes Chelm, and then goes Lublin. It used to be-- yeah, Chelm, right, the nearest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about, when you were a boy, and before the war began, do you have any memories of just being a little boy in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I used to go on cheder, learn in Yiddish. Daven in [NON-ENGLISH]-- everything, you know, it was-- sure, I remember that, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are there any other members of your family who lived in the house, or nearby that you'd like to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mention? There was-- the whole city-- not the-- the street was-- the whole street from-- all Jewish. We knew each other. It was very, very nice, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Not much Polish. But most Yiddish. Everything was in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any knowledge of anti-Semitism before the war broke out? That you-- you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right-- not before-- not way before the war, but right-- let's say, 1937, I remember, the Polacks used to went to, to the Jewish stores, and they used to scream, don't buy from a Jew. Buy from a Polack.' That was in 1937. I guess at that time, then already Hitler Germany, started to have trouble with the Jews. So I remember that. So that, that I still remember today, the anti-Semitism, yes. That was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school? Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to school, but only I finished two grades. Something happened at the public school. I went two grades because the Jewish population started to make a school on their own-- like here, like private schools. You understand? And then they took me out from the public school, and they put me in those private schools. But that private school didn't last too long, maybe two years. And then I were out, and I didn't went back to school anymore. So that's why I just finished two grades in the public, and maybe had two grades in the private school. That was all my education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or train to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After that, I-- my mother gave me-- my uncle was a tailor. And she said to me, 'You go in over there-- go in to your uncle, and you'll learn the trade as a tailor.' And that's what I did, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alta. Alta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a nice orthodox Jew, you know? And he was my own-- my, my mother's sister's husband. And he was nice, you know? I-- I learned a trade. That's what I did there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had a store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He worked for himself. Didn't have a store. Back then in Poland, was no stores like here. He worked from the house. Everybody worked from their own house. So I stayed there, maybe, for two years. And I learned a trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address you lived at in Wlodawa? Do you remember your address in Wlodawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Okulnik. 29-- I think it was-- the street's name was Okulnik.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-K-U-L-N-I-K. Okulnik. I think it was 12-- I think it was 12-- Okulnik 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned that there was obviously some anti-Semitism in the town. Did you have to wear anything special, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing special, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about after the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war broke out, that was a different story. We had to wear an armband, with a-- with a Jewish star. An armband with a Jewish star, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and whenever we saw a German walk by there, we had to kick off-- take off the hat and greet him. And that's what I remember, that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, Mr. Hipsman, when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, I was only 14 years. Right? 1925. 14 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about how and when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that it-- you see, our city is located right on the river Bug. And somehow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the Germans came in, and they parked all their tanks and everything on the-- on the place where this-- what's the name? Like, on a field-- not on a field. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, you call it on the marek. Marek. That's the old-- everybody-- like a bazaar place, you know? A bazaar. Like stands, you know. The Germans came, and-- with all their munitions, with everything, and they stayed for two weeks, and then they left. And all of a sudden, the Russian army came in. And they stayed there for a while, and they said to the old-- to the people that we should go to Russia, not stay with the Germans. And after two weeks, the Russians left. So the Russians begged us that we should go with them there, because they said in Russia, you're going to have good. Under the Germans, you don't know how it's going to be. I mean, when the Russians left a little while later, then the Germans came back. And since then, the Germans stayed till, till, till they left, till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your family in the first months of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first months of the war? Nothing, nothing special, you know? My father worked in the bakery, and life was pretty normal in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I think I did-- I, I think I started to learn my tailor, tailor profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did things change for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Changed when my father got sick in 19-- 1940. And there was no doctor. There was no nothing, you know, medicine, no nothing, and he died. In 1940, he died. Then it changed our lives because we didn't have no providers in the house for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you cope?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I cope? I'll tell you the truth-- I don't know how my mother coped. I don't know. Then I just don't know how, how she managed that. I remember a couple times she picked up some, some things, and she went in the village to sell it to make some money, or to take back some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how we survived? I don't know. We didn't have it good, you know? We, we didn't have a potato in the house to eat, you know? But I remember-- I remember, they used to cook the-- from the-- from the-- the potato peels. We used to cook it and that way, we ate. Did you help earn some money for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was too young, and I didn't-- I didn't-- in 1940, yes, I-- yeah, I remember, the first year, they used to assign Jews to go to war-- shovel snow, chop wood. So I went and took-- took over their place. And they paid me a certain amount-- let's say two zlotys, or three zlotys. And this what I did every year. That's was in 19-- 1940. And that's-- I make some money, and I give it to my mother. And she somehow coped with it. That was all summer long. Yes, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I stayed all the time with my family, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you go into the country to work at all, at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the country? That was in 19-- in 1940, 1941. So my mother took-- we were three brothers. They took us, and they put us in the village. The village is Holczov.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell Holczov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holczov is-- I think you have it there-- H-O-L-C-Z-O-O-V. Holczov. As a matter of fact, my mother was born in that village. So she knew the people there who they need some help. We should take care of the cows. And she knew a lot of people there, and they gave us-- she sent us there, and we stayed for over the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we, we were shepherds by cows. And then, they-- the guys, the people where we worked, they paid us with rye, or other things, a bit money, I don't know. My mother made the deal with them, you know? So that's how-- they bought us in a lot of-- a lot of goods to survive. How far was Holczov from Wlodawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that would be maybe, maybe 15, 18 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked from Wlodawa to Holczov?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We could walk, but we didn't walk. We came with horse and wagon took us. There was no cars at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And during the first years of the war, again, did you do anything else for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tried to once in a while. After that, when I came back from the-- be a shepherd, I went back to, to the tailor to pick up the trades again, you know? So I worked off and on as a tailor, as an apprentice. And the other hand, I tried to be [INAUDIBLE] for a living, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Wlodawa become a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Become a ghetto because-- and all around the villages-- used to be a lot of Jewish people in those villages-- send them in in Wlodawa. And we didn't know really why Wlodawa became a ghetto, until when they built Sobibor. Then we knew that was the purpose for because Sobibor was only seven kilometers from our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did Wlodawa become a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1941-- the end of 1941. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they packed in all the people from the villages. They packed them in the city. It was not enough room for everybody, so they had to squeeze in. Some-- in some-- in some houses, they lived two peop-- ten people in some. You know, we had to squeeze it in. But that's the best we could do, you know? We couldn't go out of the city. We had to stay just inside, in the city. Not go out-- out of the city. If you go out, you get killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course they were shot. Sure. Things like that-- sure, were shot. When you go out-- nobody tried to go out, you know? If you go out, you-- they, they shoot you, you know? You stay in, you stay in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you said, Mr. Hipsman, that Sobibor was very near Wlodawa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the construction of Sobibor, and how you found out about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- do you want me to tell you the story what I heard with those-- with Falkenburg at Sobibor?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Falkenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Falkenburg came to Wlodawa in 19-- in 194-- '41. And he established-- they called it the dry-- dry land. You know, we had a lot of wetland. And he came with idea to make the land dry. I think-- I started to work for Falkenburg in 1941. And in the 19-- and then in winter, we didn't work. I went back to the tailor. And then in 19-- in 1942, we just happened that I got a paper from the Yiddish gemeinde that I should go work at Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, I think, 110 people. We had to-- we had to concentrate in a place there-- it was the place-- the kino-- used to be the movie there. We came there-- all 110 people. We came there, and we didn't know what Sobibor is. We thought, going to go to work, and then a month later, we going to come back. So we went there, and we stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then all of a sudden, I saw Falkenburg came. And when he saw me, he took me out from there. He said, you, junge, come raus. So I went, and I started to work with Falkenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Mr. Falkenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German, a private German from Germany. I think he was from East Germany. We didn't know nothing else about his firm. We just knew the only thing under his name, Falkenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he doing in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- he was drying up the, the, the slums, you know? He making wetland-- out of the wetland, making dry land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was an engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess. He had another engineer there, too, with him, I remember. But he was the main, the main head of that project.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the day that you were all called in front of the cinema to gather to go to Sobibor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --had you already worked for Falkenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I started to work a few months before, when I came back as a shepherd-- from the village, as a shepherd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prepared, only, for next year, for the biggest events-- for the biggest wall, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were clearing land?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clearing, not-- measuring. Measuring, we did with the engineer, you know, measuring.  I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get paid for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the beginning, we did get paid. Small amount of money, I remember. Little, little money we got paid, yes. That helped a little, but for the house, too, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and 100 or so other people were gathered at the kino--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came there, and we were waiting till they-- somebody come and take us to Sobibor. And all of a sudden, Falkenburg arrived there, and when he saw me, he took me out from there. He said, you come out. You're going to work for me. So that's what I did. I, I went out, and I started to work with Falkenburg. Meanwhile, we didn't know what was going on in Sobibor. And the other people went back to Sobi-- went to Sobibor. But we didn't know what was going on there. But after, after a while-- after a few months later, somebody run away-- a few people run away from there. They came back to the city. And they told us what Sobibor is all about. That's why we knew what Sobibor is. And those people, what they went to Sobibor after Falkenburg took me out, they never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in your family go to Sobibor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my two brother. They took him away from the village as a shepherd in 1942. They took him out-- took him away, and they destroyed him in Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went the same time with me, as, as they took away all the workers from Falkenburg. And they, they took us to the railroad station, 194-- 1942. And somehow Falkenburg came to the railroad station. He took out 500 people. And I was one of the 500 people. And my sister, at that time, she went to Sobibor. And never saw her since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember speaking with her before she went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She said that I should see to it that Falkenburg should take me out because, she said, at least let one survivor-- let you-- you should-- you be one survivor from our family. So that was-- that was the last word I heard from her. And, she went to Sobibor, and I went to work for Falkenburg, and since then I never hear it, I never saw her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time of the year or the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year or the month? Well, it was in 1942. There were the [NON-ENGLISH.] I think it was in after the summer-- right after the summer. Yeah. Right after the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went back to work for Falkenburg--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what, what then did you do the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went back to work, and then we already digged the channels. In the beginning they measured it out, and then next year we had to dig channels, that all the water from around should go into the channels. And that's what we did every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- were there other men that you worked with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or boys, that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what I knew? I knew, I knew all of them, but now I-- I don't know them. I knew all of them. But you see, we were-- we were groups. Our group was the young, young boys groups, they used to call it. The younger groups. It was girls groups. It was men's groups. Our group, we had a leader. And our group was the young-- the young boys groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the group was maybe-- I would say, maybe 50 in the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop this tape and start another.
